ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'vegetable soup' class (FOODON_00002482); no specific Hot and Sour Vegetable Soup entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers soups and broths as a category; no specific hot-and-sour vegetable soup code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for hot and sour vegetable soup. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular food preparation codes at this level.

main_ingredients: mixed vegetables (carrot, cabbage, capsicum, mushroom, spring onion)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Hot and Sour Vegetable Soup is approximately 300 g (one bowl), as typically served in Indian restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Hot and Sour Soup (vegetable, restaurant-style) — estimated from ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 and USDA FoodData Central vegetable soup reference values
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition) and USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Indian-style Hot and Sour Vegetable Soup exists in ICMR-NIN 2017 or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated by combining ICMR-NIN mixed vegetable soup data (approximately 25–35 kcal/100 g for thin vegetable soups) with USDA FoodData Central entry for vegetable soup (FDC ID 172950, approximately 30 kcal/100 g) and adjusting for the vinegar, soy sauce, and cornstarch thickener typical of this preparation. The resulting estimate reflects a lightly thickened, low-calorie vegetable soup consistent with both sources.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of vegetable soups and mixed vegetable preparations — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for vegetable soup and mixed vegetable dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Indian-style Hot and Sour Vegetable Soup. The University of Sydney GI Database lists vegetable soups in the GI range of 30–40 (low GI). The vinegar content in this soup further suppresses postprandial glucose response (acetic acid effect documented in peer-reviewed literature: Johnston et al., 2004, JADA). A GI of 35 is used as a defensible analogous estimate. Glycemic load per serving = (35 × 5.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 300 g serving) / 100 = 5.8 g, rounded to one decimal place.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and broth
hypertension.guidance: Hot and Sour Vegetable Soup contains approximately 380 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 1140 mg per standard 300 g bowl), which represents a significant sodium load for individuals with hypertension; patients should request reduced-sodium preparation or limit portion size to half a bowl.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low carbohydrate density
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of 35 and approximately 5.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, Hot and Sour Vegetable Soup has a low glycemic load per serving and is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the vinegar content may additionally attenuate postprandial glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium from soy sauce and broth; low saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Hot and Sour Vegetable Soup is low in saturated fat (approximately 0.1 g per 100 g) and cholesterol-free, which is favourable for cardiovascular health; however, the high sodium content from soy sauce warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease, and a low-sodium version is recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hot and Sour Vegetable Soup is a popular Indian-Chinese fusion soup widely served in Indian restaurants across Singapore and India. It is prepared by simmering a medley of finely chopped vegetables — typically carrot, cabbage, capsicum, mushroom, and spring onion — in a seasoned vegetable broth flavoured with vinegar, soy sauce, green chilli, ginger, garlic, and black pepper, then lightly thickened with cornstarch to achieve a silky consistency. The soup delivers a characteristic balance of sharp sourness from vinegar and pungent heat from chilli and pepper, with a savoury umami depth from soy sauce. It is served hot and is a common starter or light meal option. Nutritionally, it is a low-calorie, low-fat preparation (approximately 32 kcal per 100 g) with moderate carbohydrate content from the thickener and vegetables. It is entirely plant-based, making it suitable for vegetarian and vegan diners. The primary clinical concern is its sodium content, which can be substantial due to soy sauce and broth; individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease should opt for reduced-sodium versions. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, the soup is generally well-tolerated given its low glycemic index (estimated GI 35) and low glycemic load per serving. The vinegar component may provide an additional postprandial glucose-attenuating effect. The soup is rich in vitamin A from carrots and provides modest amounts of vitamin C, iron, and dietary fibre.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Indian-style Hot and Sour Vegetable Soup exists in ICMR-NIN 2017 or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from the closest analogous vegetable soup entries in both approved sources, adjusted for the characteristic ingredients of this preparation. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database vegetable soup range. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN 2017 mixed vegetable soup data and USDA FoodData Central vegetable soup reference (FDC ID 172950); no exact match exists for Indian-style Hot and Sour Vegetable Soup in any approved primary source. 2. Sodium estimate of 380 mg per 100 g reflects typical restaurant preparation with soy sauce and seasoned broth; actual sodium may vary significantly between restaurants. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases for this food category and is not expected to be present in a vegetable-only soup. 4. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases for this food category. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based preparation containing no animal products; confirmed zero by formulation. 6. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based preparation; confirmed zero by formulation. 7. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as no hydrogenated fats are used in this preparation; confirmed zero by formulation. 8. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database vegetable soup range (30–40); the vinegar content may further lower the effective postprandial glucose response. 9. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (35 × 5.5 × 300) / 100 / 100 = 5.775, rounded to 5.8. 10. FoodOn URI FOODON_00002482 (vegetable soup) is used as closest parent; no specific hot-and-sour vegetable soup class exists in FoodOn. 11. FoodEx2 code A0FKP (soups and broths) is used as closest parent category. 12. This food is classified as Indian cuisine for multilingual naming purposes; mandatory local-language names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are provided per Singaporean dish rules given the food is served in Singapore.
